title: Ad Click Fraud Spike Detection
description: Detect intervals where clicks are k times higher than rolling baseline

examples.explanation: n=6. For i=0: no intervals (j must be < i). i=1: j=0 only. Interval [0,1]: avg_clicks=(10+12)/2=11. baseline=0 (j=0, no previous clicks, use default 0). Condition: 11 > 3*0? Yes, but baseline=0 is invalid (need min window). Skip. i=2: j=0: [0,2]: avg=(10+12+15)/3=12.33. baseline=0 (j=0). Skip. j=1: [1,2]: avg=(12+15)/2=13.5. baseline=10/1=10 (since j=1, previous clicks [0:0]=[10]). Condition: 13.5 > 3*10? 13.5 > 30? No. i=3: j=0: [0,3]: avg=(10+12+15+100)/4=34.25. baseline=0. Skip. j=1: [1,3]: avg=(12+15+100)/3=42.33. baseline=10/1=10. 42.33 > 30? Yes. But check min_baseline_window=2: baseline needs at least 2 points, j=1 gives only 1 previous point, so invalid. Skip. j=2: [2,3]: avg=(15+100)/2=57.5. baseline=(10+12)/2=11 (j=2, previous clicks [0:1]=[10,12]). Condition: 57.5 > 3*11=33? Yes. And baseline has 2 points ≥ min_baseline_window. Record (2,3). i=4: j=0,1,2,3: all intervals fail condition or baseline invalid. i=5: similar. Final result: [(2,3)].

n=6. i=0: none. i=1: j=0: [0,1]: avg=5. baseline=0 (j=0). Skip (baseline invalid if min_window=1? Actually min_window=1 allows single point baseline, but j=0 gives 0 previous points, baseline=0, which might be considered invalid if we require at least 1 point; assume we skip). i=2: j=0: [0,2]: avg=5. baseline=0. Skip. j=1: [1,2]: avg=5. baseline=5/1=5. Condition: 5 > 5*5=25? No. i=3: j=0: [0,3]: avg=(5+5+5+50)/4=16.25. baseline=0. Skip. j=1: [1,3]: avg=(5+5+50)/3=20. baseline=5/1=5. 20 > 25? No. j=2: [2,3]: avg=(5+50)/2=27.5. baseline=(5+5)/2=5. Condition: 27.5 > 5*5=25? Yes (27.5 > 25). Record (2,3). i=4,5: no intervals satisfy. Result: [(2,3)].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each possible interval endpoint, compute the rolling baseline by averaging previous clicks, then check if current clicks exceed k times that baseline. This straightforward approach examines all intervals but is inefficient for large datasets.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution checking all intervals against rolling baselines
ladders.explanation:
This is the complete brute force solution for detecting click fraud spikes. The intuition is straightforward: for every possible interval endpoint, we need to compute a rolling baseline from previous clicks, then check if the current clicks exceed k times that baseline. This approach examines all possible intervals systematically but inefficiently. The algorithm works by treating each index i as a potential endpoint of a fraud spike interval. For each i, we look back at all p
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

Yes, but baseline=0 is invalid (need min window). Skip. i=2: j=0: [0,2]: avg=(10+12+15)/3=12.33. baseline=0 (j=0). Skip. j=1: [1,2]: avg=(12+15)/2
... (trace simplified)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Window Sum Checker
ladders.desc: Function to determine if a specific interval's average exceeds k times its baseline
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the core checking logic used repeatedly in the brute force solution. The intuition is that for any given interval [j, i] in the clicks array, we need to compute two things: 1) The average clicks within the interval, and 2) The baseline average from clicks before the interval (specifically from index 0 to j-1). Then we check if the interval average exceeds k times the baseline. This function encapsulates the fraud detection condition. The algorithm takes as
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Indices valid (0≤2≤3<4). Step 2: Interval [2,3]: clicks[2]=15, clicks[3]=100. Sum=115, count=2, avg=57.5. Step 3: Baseline: j=2, previous indices 0 to 1. Clicks[0]=10, clicks[1]=12. Sum=22, count=2, baseline=11. Check min_baseline_window=2: count=2 ≥ 2, valid. Step 4: Compare: 57.5 > 3*11=33? Yes. Return True.

Step 1: Valid indices. Step 2: Interval [1,3]: clicks[1]=5, clicks[2]=5, clicks[3]=50. Sum=60, count=3, avg=20. Step 3: Baseline: j=1, previous indices 0 to 0. Clicks[0]=5. Sum=5, count=1, baseline=5. Check min_baseline_window=2: count=1 < 2, invalid. Return False (insufficient baseline data).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Nested Loop Subarray Exploration
ladders.desc: Generate all possible intervals [j, i] where j ≤ i
ladders.explanation:
This is the core algorithmic pattern of the brute force approach: enumerating all possible intervals in an array. The intuition is that to check every potential fraud spike, we need to consider every contiguous subarray (interval) of the clicks array. For an array of size n, there are n*(n+1)/2 possible intervals. The brute force method uses nested loops to generate these intervals efficiently. The outer loop iterates over i from 0 to n-1, where i represents the endpoint of t
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Outer loop i from 0 to 2. i=0: inner loop j from 0 to 0: interval (0,0). i=1: j from 0 to 1: intervals (0,1), (1,1). i=2: j from 0 to 2: intervals (0,2), (1,2), (2,2). Total 6 intervals.

This building block focuses on computing the average of a contiguous subarray (interval) in an array. The intuition is that for any interval [j, i], we need to efficiently compute the average of elements from index j to i inclusive. This is a fundamental operation in the fraud detection condition. The algorithm is straightforward: given an array and indices j and i, 1) Validate that 0 ≤ j ≤ i < n. 2) Initialize sum = 0. 3) Iterate from index = j to i (inclusive), adding each 
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Step 1: Indices valid (0≤1≤3<4). Step 2: sum=0. Step 3: Iterate: index=1, arr[1]=12, sum=12. index=2, arr[2]=15, sum=27. index=3, arr[3]=100, sum=127. Step 4: count = 3-1+1=3. Step 5: avg = 127/3 = 42.333333333333336.
Step 1: Valid indices. Step 2: sum=0. Step 3: index=2: arr[2]=5, sum=5. index=3: arr[3]=50, sum=55. Step 4: count=3-2+1=2. Step 5: avg=55/2=27.5.

This is a fundamental programming operation: iterating through an array while having access to both the index and the value at that index. The intuition is that in many algorithms, including our fraud detection, we need to process each element in sequence and often use the index for calculations or to define intervals. The algorithm is simple: given an array of size n, use a loop that goes from index = 0 to n-1 (inclusive). In each iteration, you can access the current elemen

index=0:5, index=1:5, index=2:5, index=3:50.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Arrays store multiple values accessible by index starting from 0.
ladders.explanation: An array is an ordered collection where each element is stored at a specific index (starting from 0). This allows systematic access and modification of elements — essential for window calculations and detecting click fraud spikes.

examples.trace: Array has 4 elements: indices 0,1,2,3. Index 2 refers to the third element (since 0-based). arr[2] = 15.
Indices: 0:5, 1:5, 2:5, 3:50. Index 3 is valid (0 to 3). arr[3] = 50.

approaches.name: Prefix Sum + Binary Search O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Use prefix sums to quickly compute click totals for any interval, then for each point, binary search to find the earliest start where clicks exceed k times the rolling baseline. This reduces interval checking from O(n²) to O(n log n) by leveraging efficient range queries.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Ad Click Fraud Spike Detection with Prefix Sum and Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution to detect intervals where clicks exceed k times the rolling baseline using prefix sums and binary search.
ladders.explanation:
The problem requires identifying all intervals [start, end] where the total clicks in that interval exceed k times the rolling baseline (average clicks per day over a window of size w). The naive O(n²) approach checks all intervals, which is inefficient for large n. Our optimized O(n log n) approach uses prefix sums to compute interval sums in O(1) time and binary search to find the earliest valid start for each endpoint. The intuition is that for a fixed end point, if we wan
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

n=5, w=3. Compute prefix: [0,10,30,60,100,150]. For end=2 (index 2): baseline=(prefix[3]-prefix[0])/3=(60-0)/3=20. Binary search start in [0,0]: mid=0, total=prefix[3]-prefix[0]=60, k*baseline*(end-start+1)=2*20*3=120, 60>120? false, left=1>right=0, break, found=-1. For end=3: baseline=(prefix[4]-prefix[1])/3=(100-10)/
... (trace simplified)

End=3: baseline=(25-5)/3≈6.67, search start in [0,1]: start=0: total=25, required=1.2*6.67*4≈32, false; start=1: total=20, required=1.2*6.67*3≈24, false. End=4: baseline=(35-10)/3≈8.33, s
... (trace simplified)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search for Earliest Valid Start
ladders.desc: For a fixed end point, find the smallest start index where interval clicks exceed a threshold using binary search.
ladders.explanation:
This subroutine is the core optimization that reduces the problem from O(n²) to O(n log n). Given an end index 'end', a threshold value 'threshold' (which is k times the baseline times interval length), and a prefix sums array 'prefix', we need to find the smallest start index in range [0, end-w+1] such that total clicks in [start, end] > threshold. The total clicks can be computed as prefix[end+1] - prefix[start]. The key insight is that as 'start' decreases (moving left), t
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: end=4, w=3, so right=end-w+1=4-3+1=2. left=0, right=2, found=-1. Iteration 1: mid=(0+2)//2=1, total=prefix[5]-prefix[1]=150-10=140, 140>100 true, found=1, right=0. Iteration 2: left=0, right=0, mid=0, total=prefix[5]-prefix[0]=150-0=150, 150>100 true, found=0, right=-1. Loop ends, return found=0.

end=5, w=3, right=5-3+1=3. left=0, right=3, found=-1. Iteration 1: mid=1, total=prefix[6]-prefix[1]=45-5=40, 40>50 false, left=2. Iteration 2: mid=2, total=45-10=35, 35>50 false, left=3. Iteration 3: mid=3, total=45-15=30, 30>50 false, left=4>right=3, break, return found=-1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Prefix Sums for Efficient Interval Sum Queries
ladders.desc: Use prefix sums to compute the sum of any subarray in O(1) time.
ladders.explanation:
Prefix sums are a fundamental technique for quickly answering range sum queries. Given an array 'clicks' of length n, we precompute an array 'prefix' where prefix[i] = sum of clicks[0] to clicks[i-1] (with prefix[0]=0). This allows us to compute the sum of any subarray [l, r] as prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]. The intuition is that prefix[r+1] gives the sum from start to r, and subtracting prefix[l] removes the sum before l. This transforms O(n) sum computations into O(1) after O(n)
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Compute prefix: prefix[0]=0; i=1: prefix[1]=0+10=10; i=2: prefix[2]=10+20=30; i=3: prefix[3]=30+30=60; i=4: prefix[4]=60+40=100; i=5: prefix[5]=100+50=150. Sum for l=1, r=3: prefix[r+1]=prefix[4]=100, prefix[l]=prefix[1]=10, sum=100-10=90.

ladders.title: Building Block — Rolling Baseline Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the average clicks over a sliding window of size w.
ladders.explanation:
The rolling baseline is the average number of clicks per day over the most recent w days. For each end index i (where i >= w-1), the baseline is computed as the sum of clicks from i-w+1 to i, divided by w. This represents the expected normal click rate. Using prefix sums, we can compute this sum in O(1) time: baseline = (prefix[i+1] - prefix[i-w+1]) / w. The intuition is that we need a moving average to compare against spikes. Steps: 1. Ensure i >= w-1 to have a full window. 
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: i=5, w=3, i-w+1=5-3+1=3. sum_window = prefix[6] - prefix[3] = 45 - 15 = 30. baseline = 30 / 3 = 15.0.

Summation is a fundamental operation where we add up all numbers in a collection. For an array 'arr' of length n, the total sum is arr[0] + arr[1] + ... + arr[n-1]. This can be done iteratively by initializing a variable 'total' to 0 and adding each element. For a subarray from index l to r, we sum only those elements. approaches.name: Monotonic Queue Sliding Window O(n)
approaches.summary: Maintain a sliding window to compute the rolling baseline efficiently using a monotonic queue to track minimum baseline values, then detect spikes when current clicks exceed k times that baseline. This approach processes data in a single pass with optimal time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Monotonic Queue Sliding Window O(n)
ladders.desc: Complete solution to detect intervals where clicks exceed k times the rolling baseline using monotonic queue and sliding window.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires identifying time intervals where ad click counts spike abnormally compared to a rolling baseline. The baseline for any time t is defined as the minimum click count in the window [t-window_size+1, t]. A spike is detected when current clicks exceed k times this baseline. The naive approach would be O(n*window_size) by scanning each window. Our optimal solution uses a monotonic queue to maintain minimum values in the sliding window, achieving O(n) time. Int
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

Now i=2 >= 2 (window_size-1), window [0,2], baseline = clicks[queue[0]]=10, 
... (trace simplified)

queue=[], result=[]. i=0: append 0 → [0]. i=1: clicks[1]=15, back has 5, 15>=5, append 1 → [0,1]. i=2: clicks[2]=10, back has 15, 10<15 so pop back → queue=[0]. Now back has 5, 10>=5, append 2 → [0,2]. i=3: clicks[3]=25, back has 10, 25>=10, append 3 → [0,2,3]. i=3>=3 (window_size-1=3), window [0,3], baseline=clicks[0]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintaining Minimum in Sliding Window
ladders.desc: Implement a monotonic queue to efficiently get the minimum value in a sliding window as it moves through an array.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the heart of the full solution. The challenge: given an array of numbers and a window size, we need the minimum value in each sliding window as we move from left to right. A naive approach would recompute min for each window in O(window_size) time, leading to O(n*window_size) total. Instead, we use a monotonic queue (double-ended queue) to achieve O(n) time. Intuition: We maintain a queue of indices such that their corresponding values are in increasing or

ladders.title: Core Logic — Monotonic Queue for Minimum Tracking
ladders.desc: Understand and implement a monotonic queue that keeps elements in increasing order to efficiently access the minimum.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic focuses on the monotonic queue data structure itself, independent of the sliding window. A monotonic queue is a double-ended queue where elements are maintained in sorted order (increasing or decreasing). For minimum tracking, we use increasing order. Intuition: By keeping only elements that are potentially the minimum in future operations, we avoid storing unnecessary larger elements. When a new element is added, we remove from the back all elements that are 
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.input: Operations: push(5), push(3), push(7), get_min(), push(2), get_min()
examples.trace: Start queue = []. 1. push(5): queue empty, append 5 → [5]. 2. push(3): back is 5, 5>=3 so pop back → queue=[]. Append 3 → [3]. 3. push(7): back is 3, 3<7 so keep, append 7 → [3,7]. 4. get_min(): front is 3 → return 3. 5. push(2): back is 7, 7>=2 so pop back → queue=[3]. Now back is 3, 3>=2 so pop back → queue=[]. Append 2 → [2]. 6. get_min(): front is 2 → return 2.

examples.input: Operations: push(4), push(4), push(1), get_min(), push(6), get_min()
examples.trace: Queue = []. 1. push(4): append 4 → [4]. 2. push(4): back is 4, 4>=4 so pop back → queue=[]. Append 4 → [4]. 3. push(1): back is 4, 4>=1 so pop back → queue=[]. Append 1 → [1]. 4. get_min(): front 1 → return 1. 5. push(6): back is 1, 1<6 so keep, append 6 → [1,6]. 6. get_min(): front 1 → return 1.

Given an array and window size, we need to find the minimum value in each window as it slides from left to right. Intuition: For each valid starting position of the window, we can scan all elements in that window to find the min. This directly solves the problem but is inefficient. ladders.title: Basic Operation — Finding Minimum in a Fixed Window
ladders.desc: Compute the minimum value in a given subarray or window of an array.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation is fundamental: given an array and a specific contiguous range (start index to end index inclusive), find the minimum value within that range. Intuition: We need to examine each element in the range and keep track of the smallest value seen. This is a simple linear scan. Step-by-step: Initialize min_val to a large number (or first element). For each index from start to end inclusive: if array[index] < min_val, update min_val. Finally, return min_val. Time
... (explanation shortened for clarity)

examples.trace: Range indices 1,2,3: values [2,7,1]. Initialize min_val = array[1]=2. Check index 2: 7 < 2? No. Check index 3: 1 < 2? Yes, update min_val = 1. Final min = 1.
Range all indices: values [10,20,30]. min_val = 10. Check index 1: 20 < 10? No. Check index 2: 30 < 10? No. Final min = 10.
